utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hi , joseph . how are you ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hey , i'm okay . good to see you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good to see you . are you ready to get started ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , i am .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . joseph is a 59 year old male here for routine follow-up of his chronic problems . so , joseph , how have you been doing ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , i've been kind of managing through my depression , and , uh , my asthma's been acting up 'cause we had a really bad pollen season , and i am at least keeping my diabetes under control , but just , uh , it's just persistent issues all around .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . well , let's start with your diabetes . so , your diet's been good ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , for the most part , but we have been traveling all over to different sports tournaments for the kids , so it was , uh , a weekend of , uh , eating on the go , crumby junk food , pizza , and did n't really stick to the diet , so that was a bit of an adjustment .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . um , but , ha- ha- have you ... let's just talk about your review of systems . have you had any dizziness , lightheadedness , fever , chills ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: running up and down the stairs , it was pretty warm , so i did feel a little bit lightheaded , and i did get a little dizzy , but i thought it was just the heat and the fatigue .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . any chest pain , shortness of breath , or belly pain ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: shortness of breath . no belly pain though .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . and , how about any joint pain or muscle aches ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , my knees hurt a little bit from running up and down , and maybe picking up the boxes , but nothing out of the ordinary .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . um , and , in terms of your asthma , you just said that you were short of breath running up and down the stairs , so , um , do , how often have you been using your inhaler over the past year ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: only when it seems to go over about 85 degrees out . that's when i really feel it , so that's when i've been using it . if it's a nice , cool , dry day , i really do n't use the inhaler .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and , um-

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and , in terms of your activities of daily living , are you able to exercise or anything like-

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , i do exercise in the morning . i , i ride , uh , our bike for probably about 45 minutes or so .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . and then , your depression , you said it's ... how's that going ? i know we have you on the , on the prozac 20mg a day . are you taking that ? are you having a lot of side effects from that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i was taking it regularly , but i've kind of weened myself off of it . i thought i felt a little bit better , but i think , uh , i , i kinda go through battles with depression every so often .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . are you interested in resuming the medication , or would you like to try a different one ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i , maybe adjusting what i'm currently taking , maybe l- less of a dose so i do n't feel the side effects as much , but i , i'm willing to try something different .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . okay , well , let's , let's go ahead and we'll do a quick physical exam . so , looking at you , you're in , in no apparent distress . i'm feeling your neck . there's no cervical lymphadenopathy . your thyroid seems not enlarged . and , listening to your lungs , you do have some bilateral expiratory wheezing that's very faint , and your heart is a regular rate and rhythm . your abdomen is soft , and uh , your lower extremities have no edema . so , let's go ahead and look at some of your results . hey , dragon , show me the pfts .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , so your , your pfts , that , those are your breathing studies , and those look quite good , so i know that you're wheezing right now , but , um , you know , i think that we can add , add , um , a regimen to that to help , to help you with your , um , exacerbations during the , the summer months , okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and then , let's look at your ... hey , dragon ? show me the hemoglobin a1c . okay , so your a1c , you're right , you know , over the past couple months is , you know , your blood sugar's probably been running a little high , so , you know , i know that you're gon na get back on your diet regimen , but , um , for right now , let's go ahead and we'll increase your metformin , okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um , and then , hey , dragon ? show me the chest x-ray . okay , good , and your chest x-ray looks fine , so we know that there's no pneumonia there .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and , it's just is all just from your asthma . so , you know , my impression of you at this time , you know , from a diabetes standpoint , let's , let's increase the metformin to 1,000 mg twice a day . um , and , we will get a repeat hemoglobin a1c in three months , and i want you to continue to monitor your blood sugars at home .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: from an asthma standpoint , let's continue you on the albuterol , two puffs , uh , every four to six hours as needed , and we'll add symbicort , two puffs twice a day during the summer , to kind of help prevent those exacerbations . and then , from a depression standpoint , we'll go ahead and start you on a different medication , zoloft , um , 25 mg once a day and see how you tolerate that . does that sound okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: perfect .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . so , the nurse will be in soon , and she'll get you situated with all of that , okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: great .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it was good to see you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: same here .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey , dragon ? finalize the note .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 59-year-old male presenting for routine follow-up of chronic conditions.
The patient reports managing depression, asthma exacerbations due to pollen, and diabetes.
Diabetes
The doctor asks if the patient's diet has been good.
The patient reports poor dietary adherence recently due to travel for children's sports tournaments.
The doctor asks about dizziness, lightheadedness, fever, and chills.
The patient reports lightheadedness and dizziness when climbing stairs, attributed to heat and fatigue.
The doctor asks about chest pain, shortness of breath, and abdominal pain.
The patient reports shortness of breath and denies abdominal pain.
The doctor asks about joint pain or muscle aches.
The patient reports mild knee pain from physical activity.
Asthma
The doctor asks about the frequency of inhaler use over the past year.
The patient uses an inhaler only when temperatures exceed 85 degrees.
The doctor asks about the patient's ability to exercise.
The patient exercises by riding a bike for 45 minutes in the morning.
Depression
The doctor asks about adherence to Prozac 20mg daily and associated side effects.
The patient reports self-weaning off Prozac.
The doctor asks if the patient wants to resume the medication or try a different one.
The patient is willing to try a different medication for depression.
Physical exam findings: no distress, supple neck, no thyromegaly, bilateral expiratory wheezing, regular heart rhythm, soft abdomen, no lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary function tests are within normal limits.
Diabetes panel shows elevated glucose and hemoglobin A1c.
Chest X-ray is unremarkable.
Increase metformin to 1000 mg twice a day.
Repeat hemoglobin A1c in 3 months and monitor blood glucose at home.
Continue albuterol 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed; add Symbicort 2 puffs twice a day; start Zoloft 25 mg once a day.
The doctor asks if the treatment plan is acceptable to the patient.
